title: practical english usage - Part 314
content:
Even most often goes with the verb, in mid-position (►  200).

He can't even write his own name.

She's rude to everybody. She's even rude to the police.

not even suggests 'less than we  expect’ .

Even suggests the idea of a surprising extreme: ’ more than we  expect’ ; 1  meaning 452 even     __________________ He took special trouble over his work.

The  adjective especial is rare. We   normally use special.

5 especial         and   special Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  453  eventually) Had you ever thought of getting married before you met Georgia?

Have you ever been to Greece?

up to now/then’ .

Ever is often used with perfect tenses (►  47, 53) to mean 'at any time 3 ever     + perfect She's the only woman ever to have climbed this mountain in winter.

It's the largest picture ever painted. He's as charming as ever.

What is the best book you've ever read?

You're looking lovelier than ever.

Ever is used in affirmative clauses in comparisons and with only.

2 with    comparatives,         superlatives,         as   and   only I'm going to stop her ever doing that again.

Nobody ever visits them. I hardly ever see my sister.

Come and see us if you are ever in Manchester.

(like nobody, hardly or stop).

We   also use ever after if, and with words that express a negative idea or I don't ever want to see you again, (   I never want.. .) Ever is possible in negative clauses, but never is more usual than not ever.

I never go to Cambridge by train. (= at no time) I sometimes go to Birmingham by train.

I always go to London by train. (= every time) Do you ever go to London by train? (= at any time) mean 'at any time’ . Compare: Ever is a ‘ non-affirmative word’  (►  222). It is used especially in questions to 1  ever     meaning       'at  any   time' 454 ever not go to America, (     Eventually I'll go to America ...) I'm not sure what I'll do next year. I could look for a job here, or I might not (     . . .-eventwl visitors.) In our new house I'd like to have a spare bedroom for possible visitors.

may, might, etc.

the idea of possibility. For this meaning we  use possible, perhaps, if, Dutch eventueel or Polish ewentualny/ewentualnie, and are not used to express They  do not mean the same as, for instance, French eventuel/eventuellement, Eventual and eventually are 'false friends’  for people who  speak some languages.

The car didn't want to start, but eventually I got it going.

The chess game lasted for three days. Androv was the eventual winner.

them to say that something happens after a long time or a lot of effort.

Eventual and eventually mean 'final(ly)’ , 'in the end’ , 'after all that’ . We   use 453 ev  entual(ly)       ___________________________ vocabulary •  456  except and except for all the rooms are clean?) not Except for the bathroom, all the rooms are clean. (     Except the bathroom, But this does not happen before all, any, nobody, etc.

Nobody came except (for) Jack and Emily.

He ate everything on his plate except (for) the beans.

I've cleaned all the rooms except (for) the bathroom.

nobody, whole, we  often leave out for.

After generalising words like all, any, every, no, everything, anybody, nowhere, 2  except        (for)   after    all,   any,    etc The garden was empty except for one small bird.

I've cleaned the house except for the bathroom.

We   generally use except for before noun phrases.

1  except        for    before     nouns 456 except       and   except       for For the difference between so and such, ►    597.

She's ever so nice.   It's ever such a good film.

These expressions are often used in informal British English to mean ‘ very’ .

455  ever     so,   ever     such For forever with progressive forms, ►    5.

For who ever, what ever, etc, ►    627.   For whoever, whatever, etc, ►    252.

I shall love you forever. I've admired him  ever since I met him.

expressions like ever after and Yours ever (used at the end of letters).

Ever also means ‘ always’  in forever (or for ever) and ever since, and in a few other evergreen trees     his ever-loving wife his ever-open mouth   an ever-increasing debt adjectives and participles.
